topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle paper felt alien under my fingertips, the ink faded to a ghostly sepia. It was tucked inside a dusty old book, a forgotten relic from a relative I barely remembered. When I finally unfolded the document, my breath hitched. It was a letter, a formal resignation, penned by my father, dated from thirty years ago. The words swam before my eyes, a sharp, unwelcome clarity: "Please accept this as formal notification of my resignation from [Company Name]." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a familiar, unpleasant clenching that spread through my chest. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp in the quiet room, and threw it into the bin as if it were contaminated. Why did I even feel compelled to look at this? It was just old paper, nothing more. Yet, the memory of my father, the sheer weight of that formal departure, settled over me like a shroud. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, my jaw tight, the air suddenly too thin to breathe normally.

[story 2]

I found the envelope tucked deep within the false bottom of an antique desk drawer. It was stiff, yellowed, and smelled faintly of mildew and forgotten time. Inside, the script was unmistakable—my mother’s handwriting, though the content was entirely unexpected. A letter of resignation. Reading it, a surge of intense irritation flooded me. It felt like a physical pressure behind my eyes, a tightening in my throat that made swallowing difficult. I stared at the date, the specific terms of the departure, and a sudden, burning resentment surfaced. This document seemed to reframe everything, painting a picture of a life I hadn't been privy to, a narrative where my parents made definitive exits. I slammed the drawer shut, the sound echoing loudly. My hands were shaking, and I needed distance. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, trying to scrub the image of that past decision from my mind, but the ink seemed permanently etched behind my eyelids.

[story 3]

The letter arrived unexpectedly, slipped into the mail addressed to my grandmother. It was my grandfather’s, written in a precise, formal hand, announcing his departure from his long-held position. As I read the words—the neat closing, the calculated language—a profound sense of antagonism rose within me. It wasn't sadness; it was a sharp, defensive recoil. My shoulders tensed, pulling inward as if bracing for an impact. I reread the passage three times, focusing on the formality, trying to dissect the coldness of the tone. It felt like an accusation, a final severing of ties that I hadn't been invited to witness. A deep furrow formed between my brows. I folded the paper with unnecessary force, the creases sharp and aggressive. I didn't want to understand it; I just wanted it gone, buried somewhere inaccessible, like a secret I was determined to keep locked away from my present reality.
